region_of_origin: Singapore (Marigold brand, F&N group; fruit sourced from Thailand, Indonesia, Philippines)
food_subgroup: Canned pineapple slices in heavy syrup (sucrose-water solution); processed tropical fruit product

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has entries for pineapple as a whole or parts; a specific canned-in-syrup format is not separately distinguished in FoodOn as of 2024. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC represents canned pineapple; heavy-syrup variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for canned pineapple in syrup as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is half a 425g can, yielding approximately 150g of pineapple slices together with their proportional share of heavy syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Marigold Pineapple Slices in Syrup — Nutrition Information Panel (Singapore product label, 425g can); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central — Pineapple, canned, heavy syrup pack, solids and liquids (FDC ID 168155)
nutrient_source.organization: Marigold Singapore / F&N (primary label data); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cross-reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and primary micronutrients taken directly from the Marigold Singapore NIP (425g can). Extended micronutrients (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, water) cross-referenced with USDA FDC ID 168155 (pineapple, canned, heavy syrup, solids and liquids) where label data was not available. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Pineapple, canned in syrup — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 (medium) for canned pineapple in syrup is directly referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 27.0) / 100 = 17.55 ≈ 17.6 per 150g serving (18.0g carbohydrate × 1.5 = 27.0g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.guidance: Canned pineapple in syrup is very low in sodium (4.5mg per 150g serving) and provides modest potassium (147mg per serving), making it a non-concern for blood pressure management in hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (17.3g/100g; ~26g per 150g serving) from both natural fruit sugars and added sucrose syrup; medium GI of 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 26g of total sugar and a GI of 65 per 150g serving (GL ~17.6), canned pineapple in heavy syrup is not recommended for T2DM patients; draining and discarding the syrup reduces sugar by approximately 30%, and juice-packed or fresh pineapple are significantly preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero trans fat, negligible sodium; high added sugar is the primary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned pineapple in syrup contains no saturated or trans fat and negligible sodium, posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk from lipid or sodium pathways; however, the high added sugar content (approximately 12–15g added sucrose per 150g serving) may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia and should be limited in patients with dyslipidaemia or metabolic syndrome.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bromelain content (anti-inflammatory enzyme); partially offset by high sugar-driven insulin response
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pineapple contains bromelain, a proteolytic enzyme with documented anti-inflammatory properties (COX-2 and NF-κB inhibition); however, heat processing during canning inactivates approximately 30–50% of bromelain activity, and the high syrup sugar content may partially offset anti-inflammatory benefit via insulin-mediated inflammation — draining the syrup is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marigold Pineapple Slices in Syrup is a widely used Singapore pantry staple sold in 425g cans by Marigold (Marigold HL Milk Pte Ltd, part of the F&N / Fraser & Neave group). The product consists of golden-yellow pineapple slices packed in heavy sucrose-water syrup, heat-sterilised during the canning process. The pineapple fruit is sourced from tropical growing regions including Thailand, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Texture is soft and tender compared to fresh pineapple; flavour is sweet with mild tropical acidity, though less bright than fresh due to heat processing. A standard serving is half a 425g can (approximately 150g including proportional syrup), providing 105 kcal, 27g carbohydrate, and approximately 26g total sugar. The critical clinical distinction is the sugar comparison with fresh pineapple: fresh pineapple contains approximately 7g sugar per 100g, while canned in heavy syrup contains 17.3g sugar per 100g — more than 2.5 times higher — due to the added sucrose syrup. Draining and discarding the syrup before consumption reduces sugar content by approximately 30%, approaching the sugar level of juice-packed alternatives. Vitamin C is reduced versus fresh pineapple (8mg/100g canned versus approximately 47mg/100g fresh) due to heat degradation during sterilisation. The product is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore cuisine, it is used as a dessert ingredient (agar-agar with pineapple, lapis sago), as a baking ingredient (pineapple tart jam filling), and as a standalone snack. For T2DM patients, always advise draining the syrup completely and preferring juice-packed or fresh pineapple. GI is 65 (medium), glycemic load approximately 17.6 per 150g serving.
common_user_queries: canned pineapple vs fresh pineapple nutrition

Marigold pineapple: มาริโกลด์ สับปะรดหั่นแว่นในน้ำเชื่อม

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the Marigold Singapore product NIP (verified product label, 425g can). Extended micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168155, pineapple canned in heavy syrup) with consistent values. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables. All values are internally consistent and source-supported.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per the Marigold Singapore NIP and USDA FDC ID 168155 — canned pineapple in syrup contains no fat by product composition (naturally zero fat fruit in sucrose-water syrup). saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero for the same reason. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived fruit product with no animal-source ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as pineapple contains no vitamin D. water_g of 80.5g per 100g is estimated from USDA FDC ID 168155 (pineapple, canned, heavy syrup, solids and liquids) as the Marigold NIP does not report moisture content. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as (GI 65 × 27.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.55, rounded to 17.6. The syrup contributes approximately 12–15g of the total 26g sugar per 150g serving as added sucrose beyond natural pineapple sugars. Draining the syrup is strongly recommended for T2DM and weight-management contexts. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest parent for canned pineapple; heavy-syrup variant not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 used as closest parent for pineapple (whole or parts); canned-in-syrup format not separately distinguished in FoodOn as of 2024.
